learning_objectives: Define organizational behavior and its contributing disciplines
Explain individual differences in perception, personality, and attitudes
Apply the Big Five personality model to workplace behavior
readings: Personality and Work Behavior Research Summary

lecture_notes.introduction: Organizational behavior studies how individuals, groups, and structures affect behavior within organizations. Understanding OB helps leaders create environments where people perform at their best.
key_concepts: OB disciplines: psychology, social psychology, sociology, anthropology
Big Five personality traits: openness, conscientiousness, extraversion, agreeableness, neuroticism
Perception and attribution: fundamental attribution error
Attitudes: job satisfaction, organizational commitment, engagement
Person-organization fit and its impact on retention
lecture_notes.demonstration: Big Five personality self-assessment and discussion of how personality profiles affect leadership style and team dynamics
setup_requirements: Textbook: Robbins & Judge, Organizational Behavior (18th ed.)

review.value: Conscientiousness is the strongest predictor of job performance across all occupations; extraversion predicts success in sales and leadership roles
Tendency to attribute others' behavior to personality traits while attributing our own behavior to situational factors

learning_objectives: Compare content and process theories of motivation
Apply self-determination theory to workplace design
Design jobs that enhance intrinsic motivation

lecture_notes.introduction: Motivation is the energy that drives behavior. Leaders who understand what motivates people can design work, rewards, and cultures that bring out the best in their teams.
key_concepts: Content theories: Maslow, Herzberg, McClelland's needs
Process theories: equity theory, expectancy theory, goal-setting theory
Self-determination theory: autonomy, competence, relatedness
Job characteristics model: skill variety, task identity, significance, autonomy, feedback
Intrinsic vs. extrinsic motivation and the overjustification effect
lecture_notes.demonstration: Job design exercise: redesigning a routine job (call center agent) using the job characteristics model to increase motivation

review.value: People are most motivated when they have autonomy (choice), competence (mastery), and relatedness (connection); external rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation
Specific, difficult goals lead to higher performance than easy or vague goals, provided there is feedback and commitment

learning_objectives: Identify stages of team development (Tuckman model)
readings: Lencioni, The Five Dysfunctions of a Team (model overview)

lecture_notes.introduction: Most work in organizations happens through teams. Understanding group dynamics helps leaders build high-performing teams and avoid common pitfalls like groupthink and social loafing.
key_concepts: Tuckman's stages: forming, storming, norming, performing, adjourning
Social loafing and free-riding: causes and remedies
Groupthink: symptoms and prevention
Team roles: Belbin's team role inventory
Psychological safety as foundation for team effectiveness (Edmondson)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Team simulation exercise: completing a complex task under time pressure, then debriefing group dynamics and decision-making quality

review.value: Team members' belief that they won't be punished for speaking up, asking questions, or admitting mistakes; Google's Project Aristotle found it was the #1 predictor of team effectiveness
Pressure for conformity suppresses dissenting opinions; prevented by encouraging devil's advocacy, seeking outside input, and creating norms for constructive conflict

learning_objectives: Identify sources of power in organizations
Apply influence tactics appropriate to different situations
readings: Cialdini, Influence: The Psychology of Persuasion (key principles)

lecture_notes.introduction: Power and politics are realities of organizational life. Effective leaders understand how to build and use power ethically to achieve organizational objectives.
key_concepts: French and Raven's power bases: legitimate, reward, coercive, expert, referent
Influence tactics: rational persuasion, inspirational appeals, consultation, coalition
Political skill: social astuteness, interpersonal influence, networking, sincerity
Ethical vs. unethical political behavior
Managing upward: influencing without authority
lecture_notes.demonstration: Power mapping exercise: analyzing influence networks in a case study organization and designing strategies for building coalitions

review.value: Most effective long-term; based on knowledge/skill (expert) and personal admiration/respect (referent) rather than position
The ability to understand others and use that knowledge to influence others to act in ways that enhance personal and organizational objectives

learning_objectives: Trace the evolution of leadership theory from traits to transformational
Evaluate authentic, servant, and adaptive leadership models
readings: George, True North: Discover Your Authentic Leadership (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: Leadership theory has evolved from asking 'who are leaders?' to 'what do leaders do?' to 'how does context matter?' Modern approaches emphasize authenticity, service, and adaptability.
key_concepts: Trait theories: intelligence, self-confidence, determination, integrity
Behavioral theories: task-oriented vs. people-oriented
Contingency theories: Fiedler, situational leadership (Hersey-Blanchard)
Transformational leadership: idealized influence, inspirational motivation, intellectual stimulation, individualized consideration
Authentic leadership: self-awareness, relational transparency, balanced processing, internalized moral perspective
lecture_notes.demonstration: Personal leadership philosophy development: reflecting on values, strengths, and leadership style to craft a written leadership credo

review.value: Transactional: exchange-based (rewards for performance). Transformational: inspires followers to transcend self-interest for the group; associated with higher satisfaction and performance
Leading from who you truly are: self-aware, transparent, ethical, and purpose-driven; builds trust and credibility over time

learning_objectives: Analyze organizational culture using Schein's three levels
Design change management strategies using established models

lecture_notes.introduction: Culture is the invisible force that shapes behavior in organizations. Leading change requires understanding culture and systematically addressing the human side of transformation.
key_concepts: Schein's three levels: artifacts, espoused values, underlying assumptions
Culture creation: founders' values, selection, socialization
Competing Values Framework: clan, adhocracy, market, hierarchy
Kotter's 8-step change model
Sources of resistance to change and strategies for overcoming each
lecture_notes.demonstration: Culture diagnosis exercise: analyzing a company's culture using Schein's framework and designing a culture change initiative

review.value: Artifacts (visible structures and behaviors), Espoused Values (stated strategies and goals), Underlying Assumptions (unconscious beliefs that truly drive behavior)
Sources include self-interest, misunderstanding, low tolerance for change, and different assessments; addressed through education, participation, facilitation, negotiation

questions.question: A tech company offers large bonuses for meeting quarterly targets, but employee engagement surveys show declining motivation and increasing burnout. Using self-determination theory, explain what might be happening and recommend changes.
questions.answer: The large bonuses may be undermining intrinsic motivation through the overjustification effect: when external rewards become the primary reason for doing work, internal drive (curiosity, mastery, purpose) diminishes. Self-determination theory suggests the company should: (1) Increase autonomy: give employees more choice in how they achieve objectives rather than prescribing methods. (2) Support competence: provide growth opportunities, skill development, and meaningful feedback rather than just outcome metrics. (3) Enhance relatedness: build team connections and link work to meaningful purpose beyond financial targets. The bonuses should be restructured as recognition rather than the primary motivation.

questions.question: A team has been together for 6 months but still struggles with open disagreement, avoids difficult conversations, and defers to the team leader on all decisions. Using Tuckman's model and Lencioni's framework, diagnose the issue and recommend interventions.
questions.answer: The team is stuck in the forming stage and has not successfully moved through storming. Using Lencioni's framework, the root dysfunction is absence of trust: team members do not feel safe being vulnerable, so they engage in artificial harmony (fear of conflict). This prevents productive debate, leading to lack of commitment (decisions without buy-in) and avoidance of accountability. Interventions: (1) Leader must model vulnerability by sharing mistakes and uncertainties. (2) Establish psychological safety norms: explicitly make it safe to disagree. (3) Use structured conflict techniques like devil's advocacy or pre-mortem analysis. (4) Conduct a team-building exercise focused on personal histories to build trust.

questions.question: A new manager relies heavily on legitimate and coercive power (ordering people and threatening consequences). Using French and Raven's framework, explain why this approach is unsustainable and recommend a power development strategy.
questions.answer: Legitimate and coercive power produce compliance but not commitment. People do the minimum required and may resist when not supervised. Coercive power breeds resentment, reduces trust, and increases turnover. These power bases also depend entirely on the manager's position; they disappear if the role changes. Recommended strategy: (1) Build expert power by developing deep knowledge in the team's domain and sharing insights that help team members succeed. (2) Build referent power by genuinely caring about team members' development, being consistent, and demonstrating integrity. (3) Use reward power to recognize and celebrate contributions rather than threaten punishment. Expert and referent power produce internalized commitment that persists without supervision.

rubric.key: Explains why current approach is unsustainable
Recommends expert and referent power development

questions.question: Compare transformational and servant leadership. In what organizational context would each be most effective?
questions.answer: Transformational leadership inspires followers through vision, intellectual stimulation, and individual consideration; it is most effective in organizations facing major change, entering new markets, or needing innovation. The leader's vision and charisma rally people around a new direction. Servant leadership prioritizes followers' needs, development, and well-being; the leader leads by serving. It is most effective in stable organizations emphasizing employee development, healthcare, education, and nonprofits where team empowerment and long-term capability building matter more than rapid transformation. Both styles build trust and commitment, but transformational is change-oriented while servant is sustainability-oriented.

major_assignments.title: Leadership Development Portfolio
major_assignments.description: Build a personal leadership development portfolio including self-assessments, leadership philosophy statement, team effectiveness analysis, and a change leadership plan for a real or simulated organizational situation.
objectives: Complete self-assessments of personality, leadership style, and emotional intelligence
Develop a personal leadership philosophy grounded in theory
Analyze a real team's dynamics and propose improvements
Design a change leadership plan using established frameworks
deliverables: Self-assessment results with reflective analysis (5 pages)
Team effectiveness analysis and recommendations (5 pages)

criteria: Honest reflection on strengths and development areas
Addresses values, style, and approach to key situations
Thoughtful self-assessment connected to OB theory
Authentic leadership philosophy grounded in values
Rigorous team analysis with practical recommendations

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of OB concepts and leadership theory

class_participation.description: Quality of contributions to discussions, exercises, and role-plays

midterm_examination.description: Covers individual behavior, motivation, and team dynamics

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam including power, leadership, culture, and change

course_policies.attendance: Mandatory for experiential exercises and team simulations. These cannot be replicated outside class.
course_policies.late_submissions: Portfolio components penalized 10% per day. Self-assessments must be completed before class discussions.
course_policies.collaboration: Leadership portfolio is individual work reflecting personal development. Class exercises are collaborative.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Self-assessments must reflect genuine responses. Fabricating assessment results defeats the purpose and is prohibited.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to personality and leadership assessment instruments. Journal or reflection tool for ongoing development notes.
